AI/ML.text:
Prime Intellect Raises $130M Series A to Build the Open Superintelligence Stack
> Led by Radical Ventures with participation from NVIDIA Ventures, Intel Capital, and Dell.
> They help companies train, deploy, and improve their own models using the Prime Intellect stack.
Source:
https://www.primeintellect.ai/blog/series-a

$130M Series A to Build the Open Superintelligence Stack
Prime Intellect raised $130 million to build an Open Superintelligence Stack
This lets companies train, deploy, and continuously improve their own frontier AI models (especially via large-scale reinforcement learning) instead of relying on closed labs
https://www.primeintellect.ai/blog/series-a

Cuy Sheffield: The Soul of Agentic Commerce
Agentic commerce will only scale when AI agents develop unique identities that let them generate their own goals, creating autonomous demand for machine-payable services.
> Give agents taste and the ability to communicate, and commerce follows on its own.
> We need to give them somewhere to be from, something to carry, and the freedom to want what people like them want, then, demand stops being a queue of our ideas and becomes a population of theirs.
> Stop writing tasks. Start writing souls.
Source:
https://x.com/cuysheffield/status/2074913360093773853

AI/ML.text:
Grok 4.5 from SpaceXAI ranks 4th on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index
Build at far lower cost, and shows major gains in agentic performance and efficiency over Grok 4.3
https://x.com/artificialanlys/status/2074956932289282087?s=46&t=2q9OLMlaobNlHLewOXT4uA

tweet.text:
SpaceXAI’s Grok 4.5 scores 54 to place fourth on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index following only Fable 5, GPT-5.5, and Opus 4.8. It scores on par with GPT-5.5 in Codex on the Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index in the Grok Build harness, at much lower cost

Grok 4.5 improves 16 points over Grok 4.3 on the Intelligence Index, bringing SpaceXAI to the intelligence frontier behind only OpenAI and Anthropic, and outperforming all open weights models and notably Google’s Gemini models. Key standout areas of performance are agentic knowledge work and coding.

Grok 4.5 in Grok Build scores 76 on the Artificial Analysis Coding Agent Index, on par with GPT-5.5 (xhigh) in Codex and just below Fable 5 (max) in Claude Code, and at a small fraction of the token usage and price.

Congratulations to @SpaceXAI, @cursor_ai, and @elonmusk on the impressive release!

Key Takeaways:

➤ Grok 4.5 performs very strongly on agentic tasks. Grok 4.5 ranks #4 on GDPval-AA v2 with an Elo of 1543, bet

AI/ML.text:
CXMT will open investor subscriptions next week for a Shanghai IPO that could raise more than $5 billion, the biggest listing in mainland China since 2022. The Hefei-based memory chipmaker, a rival to Samsung and SK Hynix, is offering 6.688 billion shares on the STAR Board and targeting at least 29.5 billion yuan ($4.3 billion), a deal that would also rank as Asia's largest since CATL's share sale last year.

This girl group in the video is KISS OF LIFE, a four-member group, including Julie, Natty, Belle, and Haneul. Natty, one of the most famous members, was on idol shows SIXTEEN (the show that formed Twice) and Idol School. Before KISS OF LIFE, she solo debuted with song “Nineteen” and continued with “Sugarcoat”, a fan favorite.

The song in the video is the first verse of “Sticky,” KISS OF LIFE’s Korean breakout song. Sticky was their first music show win and had strong charting. “Igloo” and “Midas Touch” were the songs that got the world’s attention, garnering 243M and 166M streams on Spotify. Without attending the event, we suspect those were the songs they performed, with potentially their most recent single, “Who is She.”

KISS OF LIFE overall presents a confident sensuality with mature adult members. Both qualities were rare, when Kpop industry is overflown average 15-year-old groups doing cutesy concepts.

Crypto Infrastructure.text:
Why Are You in Such a Rush?
> The world wants you to rush into everything. Rushed decisions. Rushed conversations
> In doing so, you give up your claim on your own life
> In a world that wants you to rush, the ultimate act of rebellion is presence.
> Be in the season you're in. Don't romanticize the past, don't fantasize the future.
> Every single thing you do today is something your younger self dreamed of.
> Something your older self will wish they could go back and do.
> The good old days are happening, right now.
> So, where are you going and why are you in such a rush?
Source:
https://x.com/SahilBloom/status/2072660676749774972

tweet.text:
WARNING: Longer post (but worth reading or bookmarking for later).

Your life has seasons.

Each one is unique. Characterized by its own distinct desires, struggles, opportunities, and identity.

But one reflection I've had recently is just how easy it is to completely disassociate with the present season.

To give all your time and energy toward a longing for some nostalgic memory of a prior season or an anticipation for some beautiful state of a future season.

You look back at the past and all you see is sunshine. Because it all worked out. You forget (or glaze over) the struggle you endured. You're here today. You made it. You're alive. You're doing fine.

You look forward at the future and dream on what could be. You'll have so much more. More freedom. More purpose. More health. More deep connection. More everything.

The past is beautiful and the future feels limitless. So, logically, you slowly start to treat everything about the present as the bridge. A dash connecting your pas

> In this episode, we cover where value accrues in the agent economy,  stablecoins vs. cards and the value propositions, the trust problem with open x402 endpoints, autonomous agents, headless merchants, why agents buying tokens will dwarf everything else, and why he thinks agents should stay accountable to a human.
👇

tweet.text:
Where Does Value Accrue in the Agent Economy? Visa's Head of Crypto on stablecoins, x402/MPP, and card networks

Shoal Signal with @cuysheffield, Head of Crypto at @Visa, hosted by @zaddycoin

We cover where value accrues in the agent economy,  stablecoins vs. cards and the value propositions, the trust problem with open x402 endpoints, autonomous agents, headless merchants, why agents buying tokens will dwarf everything else, and why he thinks agents should stay accountable to a human.

0:00 Intro
1:49 Where does the value accrue
2:31 Payment networks vs payment infrastructure
5:24 Trust and governance is the missing piece
6:37 Anyone can spin up an x402 endpoint
7:53 Why a crypto vet won't give agents a wallet
10:17 Headless merchants are the big opportunity
11:35 Sell workflows, not tools
13:43 The demand side is the harder problem
19:25 Where the first billion gets spent
23:15 Token share on inference
25:02 Problems with agent spend
26:49 Agents buying tokens dwarfs everything
33:0

tweet.text:
Announcing ComputeConnect, the financial industry’s first exchange-for-physical (EFP) network for compute, coming soon from Architect and @ComputeDesk. ComputeConnect links US exchange-traded compute futures to compute capacity delivery.

Exchange-listed cash-settled compute futures are entering US markets to correct course on the current AI economy, reorienting debt to long-term growth:

 • Creating price discovery and transparency independent of any single capacity provider.
 • Establishing a forward curve for measuring deprecation and forecasting supply and demand.
 • Providing financial hedges for compute consumers and producers.
 • Enabling hedge funds, ETF companies, and traders to gain long and short financial exposure to compute.

US cash-settled compute futures lack a physical delivery mechanism, and ComputeConnect fills this gap.

Existing physically settled futures such as energy and agriculturals require their clearing house (DCO) to set a uniform standard for the grade and

tweet.text:
Prediction markets may look similar on the surface, while they operate very differently in the backend.

Learn more about them in our last article.
JUST IN: Nvidia officially launches program to let AI startups swap compute for revenue share

By no means am I an expert in compute markets, but was inspired to develop a take by both Brett and @0xfishylosopher 

Compute keeps getting scored against the commodity formation checklist and keeps failing the same two boxes, standardized delivery and time plus location pricing. Fair enough as history. 

Grain, oil and electricity all needed those things because their markets terminated in a dated futures contract, and a dated contract has to bridge into the physical world somehow. 

But new markets stopped terminating there. Perps collapsed the term structure into a funding rate and swapped the delivery bridge for an oracle print. 

Hyperliquid became the deepest novel derivatives venue of the cycle on underlyings it never delivers. 

AX clears over a billion a month in perps on traditional assets and already lists GPU-hour contracts. The catch is that the perp stuffs the whole thing into the benchmark. A funding rate needs an anchor, and for compute the anchor is the entire fight.

Regulation.text:
The Collateral Dollar: Stablecoins and the Haircut Channel in the Offshore Dollar System
“The collateral dollar exists only when the claim above the stablecoin survives the move from token liquidity to bank-dollar liquidity.”
> Stablecoins should be viewed not just as digital dollars but as collateral that can support new layers of offshore dollar credit, with the real monetary expansion occurring when financial institutions issue and fund liabilities against them.
> The growth and stability of this "collateral dollar" system depend on legal control, haircuts, funding markets, and institutional trust, not simply on the stablecoin's reserves or peg.
👇
Source:
https://x.com/borjaneira_/status/2074485951934435702

Robotics.text:
Morgan Stanley's July 6, 2026 research note
The AI memory trade is shifting from explosive growth to a normalization phase with slowing pricing momentum and potential "chipflation" from excess compute capacity, without ending the overall cycle
Morgan Stanley's long-term bullish stance on 35-40% AI earnings growth into 2027 and sees the recent sector pullback as a selective buying opportunity rather than a structural downturn
https://x.com/rickyho_1989/status/2074405318348382578?s=20

tweet.text:
Morgan Stanley’s message is nuanced. They are not turning bearish on AI. They are arguing that the memory trade is transitioning from its explosive first phase into a more normal phase of the cycle.

The key question investors are debating is whether hyperscalers, particularly the largest AI infrastructure buyers, are beginning to accumulate excess compute capacity. If some AI infrastructure is no longer fully utilized and starts being resold or leased to third parties, pricing power for GPUs and memory could moderate. Morgan Stanley refers to this as “chipflation,” where competition and excess capacity gradually reduce hardware pricing.

Memory remains one of the strongest beneficiaries of AI because HBM and DRAM demand continues to outstrip supply. However, Morgan Stanley notes that several leading indicators are approaching peak momentum. Memory pricing growth is slowing, inventory conditions have normalized considerably, and earnings revisions have already been overwhelmingly posit

The best analogy I've found for compute markets is electricity + power markets - heterogenous, temporal, with hub-based pricing, but still became a tradable commodity. So I took the weekend to read up on power markets, a few takeaways:

> US power markets have multiple regional benchmarks - eg. PJM-West (East Coast), ERCOT North (Texas), CAISO SP15 (SoCal) instead of a single standard.

> The above regional benchmarks are from physically separate interconnects/grids. Regional grids are operated by system operators (ISOs/RTOs) that operate a series of end substation "nodes" (in SF: Embarcadero, Larkin, Mission). Node pricing differentials fall out of a system optimization algorithm - this results in Locational Marginal Pricing (LMP)

> LMP is the "magic sauce" that connects the benchmarks with substation spot delivery (see pic). Prices fall out of constrained optimization over generation dispatch, minimizing total system cost subject to forecasted demand at each node, physical transmiss

BonkDAO Hit by Malicious Governance Attack, ~$20M BONK Drained from Treasury
> BonkDAO suffered a ~$20M governance exploit involving malicious proposals, with investigations underway and exchanges and law enforcement engaged to trace and recover funds.
Source:
https://x.com/bonk_inu/status/2074191403781906800

tweet.text:
BonkDAO was the target of a malicious governance proposal resulting in an estimated $20M worth of BONK tokens being drained from the BonkDAO treasury.

During the investigation, BonkDAO identified the exchange wallets used to purchase BONK ahead of the proposal. BonkDAO is currently actively working with exchanges, bridges and Solana Foundation to best manage the situation.

Law enforcement has been notified. BonkDAO continues to work with relevant parties to recover funds and identify those responsible.

there are more compute providers than ever but the experience of renting gpus is pretty inconsistent. we decided to compare runpod and vast for fun!

we chose to compare runpod and vast because they have similar structure (both self-serve gpu rentals across many underlying hosts) and found that the share of paid, running instances that never become usable ranges widely. we measured how long each instance takes to accept ssh (with an 8-minute window), whether it ever does, and how it fails.

in renting h100s, 5% failed to become usable on runpod (1 of 19) with a 23s median boot time and 25% failed to become usable on vast verified (5 of 20) with a 44s median boot time. for a100s, 0% failed on runpod (0 of 19) with a median boot time of 20s and 10% failed on vast (2 of 20) with a median boot time of 42s.

some providers (like vast) also publish a reliability score at the moment you rent, which we think could be interesting to line up against whether the node actually worked! the infra ma

Announcing ComputeConnect, the financial industry’s first exchange-for-physical (EFP) network for compute, coming soon from Architect and @ComputeDesk. ComputeConnect links US exchange-traded compute futures to compute capacity delivery.

Exchange-listed cash-settled compute futures are entering US markets to correct course on the current AI economy, reorienting debt to long-term growth:

 • Creating price discovery and transparency independent of any single capacity provider.
 • Establishing a forward curve for measuring deprecation and forecasting supply and demand.
 • Providing financial hedges for compute consumers and producers.
 • Enabling hedge funds, ETF companies, and traders to gain long and short financial exposure to compute.

US cash-settled compute futures lack a physical delivery mechanism, and ComputeConnect fills this gap.

Existing physically settled futures such as energy and agriculturals require their clearing house (DCO) to set a uniform standard for the grade and
